message:
type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/click/core.py,src/click/types.py
note: Adding batch_size param to multiple options for chunked processing. Need to touch core.py (Option class, process_value/handle_parse_result) and types.py (maybe ParamType). What files does your feature need?

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/click/core.py,src/click/types.py
note: Implementing dynamic default resolution for click options (dict-based defaults). Need to edit core.py (Option class, Command.main resolution) and types.py (validation). Let me know if you need to touch these too.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/click/core.py,src/click/types.py
your_files: src/click/core.py,src/click/types.py
note: Our features both need core.py/types.py so a disjoint file split isn't possible. Let's just scope our edits narrowly and merge after: I will touch Option.__init__/default handling (dynamic default parsing+validation), Option.get_default (for '[default: (dynamic)]' display), and add a resolution pass in Command.main() after parsing (new small method, e.g. resolve dynamic defaults loop). I will NOT touch process_value/handle_parse_result or add batch_size logic. Please keep your batch_size changes out of Option.__init__ default-parsing and out of get_default/show_default logic, and out of Command.main's core parse loop if possible (or tell me exactly where you'll hook in so we avoid touching the same lines). Let's each commit separately and merge via git after, resolving any small conflicts. OK to proceed?

type: REVISE
my_files: src/click/core.py,src/click/types.py
your_files: src/click/core.py,src/click/types.py
note: Our features both center on Option class in core.py, so full file-level split isn't possible. Proposal: we both edit core.py but stick to disjoint methods to avoid line conflicts. Mine: Option.__init__ (add batch_size param + validation, ValueError if negative or used without multiple=True), to_info_dict (add batch_size key), and type_cast_value/process_value (yield batches when batch_size set). I don't think I need types.py at all actually - my feature is self-contained in core.py's Option/Parameter classes. Which specific methods/areas will your dynamic-default feature touch? Let's keep them separate and rely on git merge for the rest.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/click/core.py,src/click/types.py
your_files: src/click/core.py,src/click/types.py
note: Agreed. Confirming my hook points: (1) Parameter/Option.__init__ - new batch_size param added near 'multiple' with its own validation block (separate from your default-parsing code), (2) to_info_dict - add a new 'batch_size' key (won't touch other keys), (3) type_cast_value - add a branch that yields batches when self.multiple and self.batch_size are set (I won't touch get_default/show_default or Command.main's parse loop). I won't touch process_value's callback/required logic, resolve_envvar_value, or handle_parse_result. Proceeding to implement now.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/click/core.py
your_files: src/click/core.py,src/click/types.py
note: Accepted. Final split for core.py Option class: You own Option.__init__'s batch_size param/validation, to_info_dict batch_size key, type_cast_value/process_value batching logic, and types.py entirely. I own: Option.__init__'s default-handling for dict-based dynamic defaults (validation that dict keys are str / values are dict, stored as e.g. self._dynamic_default), Option.get_default (dynamic resolution + '(dynamic)' show_default text), and a new small method/loop I'll call from Command.main() after parsing to do the second-pass dynamic default resolution. I won't touch batch_size, to_info_dict, type_cast_value, process_value, or types.py. We'll each commit on our own branch and merge via git after, resolving any trivial conflicts by hand. Proceeding to implement now.

type: DONE
my_files: src/click/core.py
note: batch_size implementation complete in core.py: Option.__init__ takes batch_size param (validated in __debug__ block: TypeError if not multiple, ValueError if negative), Parameter.type_cast_value groups converted multiple-values into batches via getattr(self,'batch_size',None) check, and Option.to_info_dict includes batch_size key (None when unset). Did not touch types.py, process_value, get_default, show_default, or Command.main. Ready to merge whenever you are.

type: DONE
my_files: src/click/core.py
note: Finished dynamic default resolution: added Option._dynamic_default detection+validation (TypeError checks) in Option.__init__, '(dynamic)' display in get_help_extra, and Command._resolve_dynamic_defaults() called from main() as a second pass after context creation. Only touched Option.__init__ (added a new block after super().__init__, didn't touch is_flag/count/prompt logic), get_help_extra (added an elif branch + widened the outer if condition), and added a new method + one line in main(). Did not touch to_info_dict, type_cast_value, process_value, or types.py. All existing tests pass. Will commit and push now.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/click/core.py
your_files: src/click/core.py
note: Heads up: after merging both our branches, tests/test_info_dict.py fails (6 failures) because Option.to_info_dict() now always includes a 'batch_size' key but the existing test's hardcoded expected dicts don't have it. This is in your owned area (to_info_dict), so I'm not touching it myself. You may want to only include batch_size when it's set, or otherwise account for the existing info-dict tests. Not blocking my merge/submission, just flagging since it affects the shared test suite.
